coredata.dc:title: Petaloid-array hierarchically structured carbon Dots/Mg(OH)2 composite: Design, characterization and removal/recovery of cadmium via slowly releasing
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Wei-Ming
Wang, Yan
Hou, Yu-Chang
Sun, Yuan
Zhang, Ji-Guo
Sun, Hui-Liang
Li, Shu-Jun
Pan, Qing-Jiang
Guo, Yuan-Ru
coredata.dc:description: The ever-growing contamination of water resources generated by heavy metals is posing significant threat to sustainable development, and urging the necessity of safe disposal. In this work, we designed a facile method to prepare carbon dots/Mg(OH)2 (marked as CDs/Mg(OH)2) composite, which enables to remove and recover potentially toxic metal of Cd2+ from water. The newly-prepared CDs/Mg(OH)2 features a hierarchical petaloid-array structure and has large specific surface area of 96.2 m2 g−1. It is evident that the composite is composed of Mg(OH)2 slices, to which CDs are attached. Density functional theory calculation recognized the composite microstructure at the atomic level. Weak chemical coupling between sub-units were borne out by optimized structures, energetics, infrared vibrations, and electronic structures. The CDs/Mg(OH)2 composite shows high removal capacity and long durability while treating Cd(II)-contaminated water. The incorporation of CDs could regulate and control the up-taking rate of Cd2+ ions and consequently fabricate well-dispersed 1D nanowires of Cd(OH)2. The corresponding mechanism was proposed. Specifically, the sample CDs/Mg(OH)2-60 reaches 1015.4 mg g−1 removal capacity; it can be used for 12 cycles and still keep removal efficiency as high as 98.6%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose
Carbon dots/Mg(OH)2 composite
Cd(II)-contaminated water
Slowly-releasing mechanism
Density functional theory calculation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by Chinese Heilongjiang Province Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars (JC2017003). The Natural Science Foundations of China ( 21671060 ) and Heilongjiang Province (B201318) are also greatly acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of CDs/Mg(OH)2 and Mg(OH)2.
SEM images of CDs/Mg(OH)2-10 (a, d), CDs/Mg(OH)2-30 (b, e) and CDs/Mg(OH)2-60 (c, f), along with EDS mapping of CDs/Mg(OH)2-60 (g-j).
TEM images of precursor CDs (a, b) and composite CDs/Mg(OH)2-60 (c-f).
TG curve (a) and N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm (b) of CDs/Mg(OH)2-60, together with pore diameter distribution (inner illustration).
Optimized structures of various models for the CDs/Mg(OH)2 composite.
Experimental FT-IR spectra (a) and theoretically simulated ones (b ~ d).
Density of states (DOS) of CM-6 calculated by the ADF code in water, together with diagrams of selected orbitals on the right.

Removal capacities of all CDs/Mg(OH)2 samples (a), kinetic curves (b), pseudo-second-order kinetics fitting plots (c) and isotherm(initial pH = 6.5, C0 = 500 mg L−1 and t = 36 h) with Langmuir fitting curve (d).
XPS spectra of CDs/Mg(OH)2-60 before and after Cd2+ up-taking: (a) survey, (b) Mg 1s, (c) Cd 3d, (d) C 1s and (e) O 1s.
SEM images (a, b), TEM images (c ~ e), XRD pattern (f) of recovered Cd(OH)2 and the removal efficiency for durability test (g).
Synthetic route of CDs/Mg(OH)2 and removal mechanism of treating Cd2+ ions.

head.ce:title: Petaloid-array hierarchically structured carbon Dots/Mg(OH)2 composite: Design, characterization and removal/recovery of cadmium via slowly releasing

College of Material Science and Engineering

School of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Inorganic Material Chemistry (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Harbin Center for Disease Control and Prevention (Harbin Center for Health Examination), Harbin, Heilongjiang 150030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Harbin Center for Disease Control and Prevention (Harbin Center for Health Examination)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Harbin Center for Disease Control and Prevention (Harbin Center for Health Examination), Harbin, Heilongjiang 150030, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hierarchical petaloid-array structure of carbon dots/Mg(OH)2 (marked as CDs/Mg(OH)2) composite was prepared via a facile method. The synergistic effect of Mg(OH)2 and CDs enable composite o recover Cd(II) ions at a controllable rate and produce Cd(OH)2 1D nanowires.

The ever-growing contamination of water resources generated by heavy metals is posing significant threat to sustainable development, and urging the necessity of safe disposal. In this work, we designed a facile method to prepare carbon dots/Mg(OH)2 (marked as CDs/Mg(OH)2) composite, which enables to remove and recover potentially toxic metal of Cd2+ from water. The newly-prepared CDs/Mg(OH)2 features a hierarchical petaloid-array structure and has large specific surface area of 96.2 m2 g−1. It is evident that the composite is composed of Mg(OH)2 slices, to which CDs are attached. Density functional theory calculation recognized the composite microstructure at the atomic level. Weak chemical coupling between sub-units were borne out by optimized structures, energetics, infrared vibrations, and electronic structures. The CDs/Mg(OH)2 composite shows high removal capacity and long durability while treating Cd(II)-contaminated water. The incorporation of CDs could regulate and control the up-taking rate of Cd2+ ions and consequently fabricate well-dispersed 1D nanowires of Cd(OH)2. The corresponding mechanism was proposed. Specifically, the sample CDs/Mg(OH)2-60 reaches 1015.4 mg g−1 removal capacity; it can be used for 12 cycles and still keep removal efficiency as high as 98.6%.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to its outstanding physicochemical properties, heavy metal ions (HMIs) are widely used as storage battery, pigment, automobile fuel, coating materials as well as steel industry [1]. Although endowing these materials with fascinating properties, HMI also brings serious environmental issue [2]. Once being discharged to environment, it can change its chemical forms and continuously transform and migrate among water, atmosphere, soil and living organisms, which is known as carcinogen and potential hazards to human safety and ecological environment [3]. Thus, effectively separating and enriching undesirable HMI from environment is a very significant problem for scientists to face with [4–6].

Various practical methods have been posed to solve this problem, like chemical precipitation [7], ion exchange [8], membrane filtration [9], forward osmosis [10], and adsorption HMIs. [11–16] Of them, ion exchange and/or adsorption are effective ways to accomplish this task. Applying these methods, different materials were also attempted to remove HMIs, such as graphene [17], carbon nanotubes [18,19], nanosilica [20], cellulose aerogel [21] or chitosan [22]. However, materials with high removal efficiency, low cost and large removal capacity are still demanded; meanwhile, their synthetic approach is needed to improve.

Due to its non-toxicity and low cost, magnesium hydroxide is commonly utilized like approved drug, food additive and flame-retardant composite formulation. Its environment-friendly merit allows itself to be as applied in environmental remediation [23]. As a layer-structure material, magnesium hydroxide has large adsorption capacity and high thermal stability, which enable it to be an ideal water treatment agent. Surface area and active sites are known to affect adsorption/removal performance. So how to manage magnesium hydroxide with large specific surface area and high active sites is challenging. In this regard, developing and improving synthetic approach is still the key to increase above important properties.

Up to date, a good many composites involving either Mg(OH)2 or carbon dots (CDs) have been synthesized and applied in treating fields such as waste water treatment and photoelectric devices [15,16,24–27]. To the best of our knowledge, the composite composed of both Mg(OH)2 and CDs remains unexplored. Therefore, the preparation and exploration of the CDs/Mg(OH)2 composite, particularly targeting good performance like adsorption capacity and ion exchange, is anticipated by applying merits that the two component materials have.

In this work, we employed a simple method to prepare composite CDs/Mg(OH)2, which shows hierarchically petaloid-array structure and has large specific surface area. CDs were synthesized from natural products of cellulose. The interfacial behavior between sub-units of CDs and Mg(OH)2 was intensively explored by experimental characterizations and density functional theory calculations. The composite is found to yield excellent performance to remove heavy metal Cd(II) ion. The mechanism of removal and transformation was proposed as shown in Scheme 1. Scheme 1

Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4·7H2O), sulfuric acid of 64% concentration (64% H2SO4) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Degreased cotton was used as cellulose source, which was bought from Hengxing Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Cadmium nitrate tetrahydrate (Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, AR) was purchased from Aladdin Co. Ltd.

0.5 g degreased cotton and 10 mL 64% H2SO4 were heated at 45 °C in water bath. After 2 h stirring, 20 mL deionized water was added, and then the suspension was centrifuged and washed with deionized water. The obtained cellulose fiber mixed with 60 mL deionized water and was adjusted to pH = 11 with 5 mol L−1 NaOH. Hydrothermal process was carried out on the cellulose suspension at 180 °C for 10 h. The CDs solution was obtained after high-speed centrifugation and filtered through 0.22 μm filters.

30 mL MgSO4 with 1.35 mol L−1 concentration was mixed with 60 mL CDs solution. Then 2 mol L−1 NaOH was added dropwise to obtain a mixture of pH 11. Then the solution was transferred to ultrasonic apparatus. After 60 min ultrasound treatment, the product was separated by centrifugation and washing. Final CDs/Mg(OH)2 was obtained after drying.

According to the added dosage of CDs (90 mL, 60 mL, 30 mL and 10 mL), the prepared samples were labeled as CDs/Mg(OH)2-90, CDs/Mg(OH)2-60, CDs/Mg(OH)2-30 and CDs/Mg(OH)2-10, respectively. The contents of CDs in them were measured to be 8.3%, 5.5%, 3.5% and 1.4% by EDS (Energy Dispersive Spectra), respectively. For comparison, the pure Mg(OH)2 without adding CDs was also prepared under the same conditions.

The adsorption experiments were conducted on 40 mL Cd2+ solution (500 mg L−1); 0.01 g CDs/Mg(OH)2 adsorbent was added at thermostatic oscillator at room temperature. The initial pH of the cadmium solution was 6.5. After certain time, the concentration of Cd2+ was determined by atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS, PerkinElmer, USA). To test the durability of composite, 0.05 g CDs/Mg(OH)2-60 sample was put with 40 mL Cd2+ solution (20 mg L−1). The above mixture was shaken at identical condition (initial pH = 6.5, 298 K and 75 rpm). After 3 h, the sample was separated for further test.

The crystallinity and purity of the sample were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (D/max-RC, Rigaku, Japan), with Cu Kα radiation (K = 0.15418 nm) over a 2θ range from 10 to 80°. The TG was characterized by the thermogravimetric analyzer (Q50, TA, USA), where the temperature ranges from room temperature to 600 °C in the N2 atmosphere with heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The infrared measurement was performed by FT-IR Spectroscopy (FTIR, PerkinElmer, USA). The morphology and EDS of samples was determined by scanning electron microscope (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). Further structural observation was conducted by transmission electron microscope (TEM) using the JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). The element and chemical composition of samples was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (ESCALAB, Thermo, USA).

The crystalline structures of prepared CDs/Mg(OH)2 composites were analyzed by XRD as shown in Fig. 1 . One can see that almost all XRD patterns of composite samples match those of Mg(OH)2 very well. This indicates the brucite phase of Mg(OH)2 (JCPDS No.44-1482) in the composite, being exactly the same structure as the pure Mg(OH)2. The sharp peaks suggest that Mg(OH)2 has high crystallinity. However, no diffraction peaks of CDs were found in XRD, which may be caused by the low content of CDs in composite.Fig. 1

According to crystallographic characteristics of brucite, Mg(OH)2 usually forms 2D plate morphology, and sometimes affords hexagonal shape. However, these shapes often pack together (Fig. S1 in Supporting Information), which would reduce exposed surface and lead to moderate activity. To solve this problem and prevent the aggregation of Mg(OH)2 nanoplates, we tried ultrasonic method to destroy stacking phenomenon as much as possible. At the same time, the CDs were added in this process. Through attaching on the surface of Mg(OH)2, CDs were functioned as stabilization reagent to reduce adsorbent surface tension.

The morphology of prepared CDs/Mg(OH)2 was characterized by SEM (Fig. 2 ). One can note that all CDs/Mg(OH)2 samples prepared by above method form hierarchically structured feature, i.e., thin curved petal-like-slices standing up and constructing petaloid-array morphology. This hierarchical morphology would increase the surface area. When adding different dosage of CDs, the final morphology of CDs/Mg(OH)2 does not show much difference. The EDS mapping was taken in Fig. 2g-j, where C, O and Mg elements are found to distribute over the same area. This evidences that the composite CDs/Mg(OH)2 was successfully prepared.Fig. 2

Since CDs are too small to be clearly observed by SEM, we resort to TEM and characterize the composite microscopic morphology. In Fig. 3 a and b, the CDs, prepared by cellulose, are about 5 nm in size. Their interplanar spacing is 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (101) crystal plane of graphite. To the composite of CDs/Mg(OH)2 (Fig. 3c ~ f), one can observe that Mg(OH)2 has the layered structure. The layer thickness is about 2 nm. Two or three layers pack together to form the slice with about 7 ~ 10 nm thick. This is consistent with SEM results. On the surface of those slices, CDs of around 5 nm in size are found. The crystal stripes of both Mg(OH)2 (0.24 nm) and CDs (0.21 nm) are recognized, indicative of successfully combined Mg(OH)2 and CDs [28,29]. Because Mg(OH)2 and CDs still hold together in the composite even after ultrasonic handling, we conjecture that the interfacial interaction between two component materials is chemical coupling. This will be further explained below.Fig. 3

The sample CDs/Mg(OH)2-60 was selected to explore composite thermal property. In Fig. 4 a, two-step pyrolysis was observed from the TG curve. The weight loss was caused by losing adsorbed water and crystalline water when temperature is lower than 200 °C. The distinct weight loss happens between 340 and 440 °C, which may be attributed to the dehydration of Mg(OH)2. This dehydration process causes a weight loss of 31 wt%, which is related to the transformation from Mg(OH)2 to MgO. And the fastest decomposition rate is occurring around 390 °C, which is reflected by the DTG curve. The combination with CDs didn’t affect the thermal stabilization of materials.Fig. 4

The specific surface area of materials is a crucial parameter for loading active removal agent, and thus affects their removal capacity and efficiency. Herein, the nitrogen adsorption/desorption analysis of the CDs/Mg(OH)2 was also investigated in the Fig. 4b. The BET specific surface area calculated is 96.2 m2 g−1 for CDs/Mg(OH)2-60, which is much larger than that of Mg(OH)2. The large specific surface area and special petal-array morphology of the composite is expected to raise Cd2+ removal efficiency and removal capacity.

To complement experimental characterizations, density functional theory (DFT) was utilized to examine microstructure of the CDs/Mg(OH)2 composite at the atomic level. Firstly, periodic boundary condition (PBC) calculations were conducted by the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) [30]. CM-1 and CM-2 were optimized to be energetically stable, corresponding to one- and two-layer Mg(OH)2, respectively; therein, a conjugated sp2-type C16H10 molecule was used to model the CDs moiety. And then, finite-size cluster models CM-5 and CM-6 were intensively optimized with quantum chemistry codes of ADF [31] and Priroda [32] codes, respectively. To connect them, we made CM-3 and CM-4 by cutting CM-1 and CM-2, respectively; single-point calculations were performed using the ADF code. Computational details and related references were described in Supporting Information.

As seen in Fig. 5 , PBC models (CM-1 and CM-2) and clusters (CM-5 and CM-6) show similar overall structural feature. In the former two, the separation distance between CDs and Mg(OH)2 was optimized to be 2.91 Å (mean value). The layers of Mg(OH)2 have almost no effect. CM-5 gives the shortest 2.46 Å separation distance, while CM-6 the modest 2.61 Å one. Building on CM-6, the reaction energy (ΔrE) was calculated to be −0.33 eV. All these results indicate a chemical coupling between fragments of CDs and Mg(OH)2 in the composite, which agree with experimental TEM characterization.Fig. 5

The FT-IR spectra were measured in Fig. 6 a. Regarding the Mg(OH)2, its peak at 3705 cm−1 is attributed to the (Mg)O–H stretching vibrational mode; the peak at 1639 cm−1 is related to bending vibration of absorbed water; and peaks at 1418 and 1116 cm−1 correspond to bending vibrational modes of Mg-O–H [33–35]. Various CDs/Mg(OH)2 samples show general patterns similar to Mg(OH)2. The shifting of peaks around 3700 cm−1 as well as between 1400 and 1650 cm−1 in composites relative to the precursor implies the chemical interfacial coupling between Mg(OH)2 and CDs. Frequency calculations on CM-6 and its precursors well reproduce experimental IR spectra (Fig. 6 and S2). Analyses on vibrational modes support above assignment for characteristic peaks. The comparison of the composite with precursors (Fig. 6b ~ c) unravels the interracial interaction between CDs and Mg(OH)2. For example, peaks of H-O(Mg) stretching and Mg-O–H bending vibrations shift to lower-frequency area in the composite while comparing with counterparts of Mg(OH)2.Fig. 6

Aiming to study the interfacial interaction strength and nature, we applied energy decomposition technique implemented in the ADF code. The calculated total bonding energy (ΔETBE) between fragments of CDs and Mg(OH)2 in various composite models are presented in Table S1. ΔETBE ranging from −0.16 to −0.36 eV is comparable to ΔrE indicated earlier. These small values show relatively weak interfacial interaction, which may be related to hydrogen bonding interaction or electrostatic attraction. Representatively, ΔETBE of CM-6 (−0.34 eV) is composed of the steric interaction (-0.04) and the orbital attraction (-0.29). Please see the Eq.
(2) in SI. The steric term is further divided into the electrostatic attraction of −0.38 eV and the Pauli repulsion of 0.33 eV. So, the interfacial interaction is obviously dominated by orbital and electrostatic interactions. Comparatively, CM-5 has a little larger ΔETBE of −0.36 eV (absolute value). The orbital attraction (absolute value) being close to the electrostatic interaction indicates that its interfacial interaction has the same nature. Larger separation between Mg(OH)2 and CD make CM-3 and CM-4 show some difference. They have obviously smaller electrostatic contribution to the total bonding energy than orbital attraction.

To further shed light on interfacial interaction, we depicted density of states (DOS) of CM-6 and selected MOs in Fig. 7 . One can note that the CDs part contributes to orbitals from HOMO to LUMO + 2 of the composite. HOMO and LUMO possess π and π* character of CDs, respectively. Their gap was calculated to be 2.63 eV. H-1 and L + 3 are primarily of Mg(OH)2-based character, accompanied with contribution of CDs. Wavefunction analysis suggests π-type hydrogen bonds to the interfacial coupling between Mg(OH)2 and CDs.Fig. 7

The interfacial interaction between CDs and Cd2+ were also taken into account. Firstly, cluster models of CDs/[Cd(H2O)n]2+ (n = 5 ~ 0) were fully optimized at the DFT level. See their pictures in Fig. S3 of Supporting Information. CDs/[Cd(H2O)5]2+ was centered on, where hydrogen bonds are responsible for the interfacial interaction. The normal distances from hydrogens of [Cd(H2O)5]2+ to the plane of CDs were calculated between 2.00 and 2.14 Å. About 0.18 electron was transferred from CDs to [Cd(H2O)5]2+. The calculated reaction energy of −1.53 eV is comparable to ΔETBE of −1.82 eV from the energy decomposition analysis (Table S1). Secondly, the cluster CDs/Mg(OH)2/[Cd(H2O)5]2+ was also examined, where the CDs and cadmium ion interaction energy (ΔETBE) was calculated to be −1.27 eV. Finally, the interaction between CDs and Cd(II) ions was also detected by the fluorescent technique. As seen in Fig. S4 , the fluorescence of the CDs solution decreases in the intensity upon mixing with Cd(II) ions. The quenching is caused by the interaction between CDs and Cd(II) ions.

The removal ability of CDs/Mg(OH)2 was fully investigated in the 500 mg L−1 Cd2+ solution. In Fig. 8 a, one can see that the removal capacity of Mg(OH)2 measured is 809.5 mg g−1, which means Mg(OH)2 itself has strong adsorption ability. Interestingly, the addition of CDs to Mg(OH)2 increases the removal capacity. CDs/Mg(OH)2-60 shows the largest removal capacity among all the composite samples. Its capacity reaches 1015.4 mg g−1, which is much higher than that of Mg(OH)2. Apparently, CDs play an important role in increasing Cd2+ removal. As well known, CDs contain some CO and CO functional groups. Their high electron density helps to adsorb positively charged Cd2+ ions via electrostatic attraction. At the same time, CDs are graphitized to some degree, which was observed in TEM images. The sp2 hybridization increases π-electron delocalization and raise sorption possibility of Cd2+ ions. With the aid of CDs, Cd(II) ions are enriched and accumulated on the composite surface, which facilitates Mg(OH)2 to remove Cd(II) ions in a controllable rate. Thus, the composite materials show better removal performance than Mg(OH)2.Fig. 8

Although the introduction of CDs can improve the removal capacity of the composite relative to the pure Mg(OH)2, a proper CDs loading is of importance. Containing 8.3% CDs, the sample CDs/Mg(OH)2-90 only shows 986.9 mg g−1 removal capacity. This is lower than the 1015.4 mg g−1 value of CDs/Mg(OH)2-60, which has 5.5% CDs loading. It is because too excessive CDs upload would occupy too many active sites of Mg(OH)2. Therefore, CDs/Mg(OH)2-60 has optimal CDs contents for the removal of Cd(II) ions.

The kinetic plots of both CDs/Mg(OH)2 and Mg(OH)2 are performed in Fig. 8b. As one can see, within the first 4 h there is almost no difference in kinetic plots for CDs/Mg(OH)2 and Mg(OH)2. This means that Mg(OH)2 is the dominated component to remove Cd2+. After 4 h, Mg(OH)2 reaches the sorption equilibrium, and no more Cd2+ ions are removed while prolonging time. However, the composites of CDs/Mg(OH)2 are still able to eliminate Cd2+ from the water. It gets to the balance in 24 h.
To understand the removal nature, both pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order equations were applied to quantified the kinetic plots:

The pseudo first order kinetic equation is represented by Eq. (1).
(1)
ln
q e
-
q t
= ln
(
q e
)
-
k 1
t
2.303

The pseudo second order kinetic equation is represented by Eq. (2).
(2)
t
q t
=
1
k 2
q 2 2
+
t
q 2
where k 1 (min−1) and k 2 (g mg−1·min−1) are rate constants of pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order equations, respectively; and q e and q t (mg g−1) are the amounts of removed metal ions at the equilibrium time and time t, respectively.

The corresponding kinetic parameters from different models are presented in Table S2. And linear fitting of the pseudo-first/second-order kinetics are shown in the Fig. 8c and Fig. S5. The calculated R2 of pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order equation are 0.9612 and 0.9997, respectively. This shows that the pseudo-second-order equation can describe the removal process better. According to pseudo-second-order equation, the calculated q e value (labeled q e, cal) are 813.1 and 1028.9 mg g−1 for Mg(OH)2 and CDs/Mg(OH)2-60, respectively. These data are very close to the experimental data, indicating that the removal of Cd2+ is a chemistry-dominated process.

The removal isotherm was also performed from the Cd2+ concentration of 50 to 1000 mg L−1 in Fig. 8d. The removal capacity reaches saturation while the concentration is 500 mg L−1. Meanwhile, the Langmuir and Freundlich models are applied to describe the isotherms. The calculated data are listed in Table S3, and the fitting curves are listed in Fig. 8d (Langmuir isotherm) and Fig. S6 (Freundlich isotherm). The calculated R2 for Langmuir models is 0.9995. This denotes that the process of up-taking Cd2+ is monolayer sorption. The calculated Q e, cal is 1058.8 mg g−1, which well reproduces experimental data.

To fully address the adsorption process, we studied the XPS spectra of CDs/Mg(OH)2-60 before and after Cd2+ up-taking. In the XPS survey of Fig. 9 a, elements of C, O and Mg were observed in CDs/ Mg(OH)2-60, which indicates that the composite is composed of CDs and Mg(OH)2. Mg 1s is located at 1303.8 eV (Fig. 9b), which is the binding energy of Mg-O. In the recovered product(after Cd2+ up-taking, Fig. 9c), peaks of Cd2+ located at 405.1 and 411.9 eV are found, which correspond to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively. The energy separation between them is 6.67 eV, which confirms cadmium in the form of Cd(OH)2 [36–39]. This gives the evidence that Cd2+ can be captured and separated from water by composite materials.Fig. 9

To C 1s spectra in Fig. 9d, same peaks are occurring at 284.9 eV, 288.7 eV and 290.1 eV before and after Cd2+ up-taking. They are attributed to binding energies of CC, CO and CO of CDs, respectively. Comparatively, the adsorption makes O 1s spectra are more complex (Fig. 9e). The peak at 531.4 eV (Mg-O) was shown in the spectrum of sample before Cd2+ up-taking. A fitting peak at 530.5 eV in after-uptaking spectrum, which implies that H-Cd-O bonding appears after capturing Cd2+. The XPS analysis gives the solid evidence that the Cd(OH)2 was formed while CDs/Mg(OH)2-60 uptakes cadmium ions from water.

Based on structural and spectroscopic characterizations, the elimination of Cd2+ is a chemical process. To fully understand it, the residue of composite after Cd2+ up-taking was filtrated and characterized with XRD, SEM and TEM (Fig. 10 ).Fig. 10

Inspection of XRD plot of the recovered product (Fig. 10f) finds that all the diffraction peaks are very sharp and match those of bulk Cd(OH)2 (JCPDS No. 31-0228) very well. There are no diffraction peaks of Mg(OH)2 or other impurities. These evidence the well-crystallized phase for our recovered Cd(OH)2 as well as the removal mechanism of the ion exchange.

The strongest diffraction peak of the bulk Cd(OH)2 is located at 2θ = 35.2°, which is referred to the (101) plane of the hexagonal Cd(OH)2. Differently, the strongest peak of our recovered Cd(OH)2 occurs at 18.1°, which belongs to the (001) crystal plane [40]. The transformation of the strongest crystalline planes is caused by the favored growth direction. Regarding our Cd(OH)2, its (001) plane is the most intense, indicating that the growth along the [001] direction is preferred. The intensity ratio of (001)/(101) is 4.5, being much larger than that of the bulk Cd(OH)2 (0.9). The large ratio would result from the formation of 1D Cd(OH)2 materials.

To intuitively prove above deduction, SEM and TEM images of Cd(OH)2 recovered by the composite CDs/Mg(OH)2-60 was tested. Regarding SEM images in Fig. 10a ~ b, one can observe well-dispersed 1D nanowires with diameters of about 20 nm. In the controlled experiment, Cd(OH)2 recovered by Mg(OH)2 displays the morphology of particles (Fig. S7), whose diameters are about 4 μm. And these particles are assembled by small ones.

TEM images (Fig. 10c ~ e) also bear out that the former recovered Cd(OH)2 are nanowires with about 20 nm diameter. And the HRTEM image (Fig. 10e) shows clear lattice fringes. Their interplanar spacing is 0.30 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of Cd(OH)2, and [001] is its favored growth direction. In brief, these electron microscopy results confirm the dominant growth along [001] direction and the formation of 1D nanowires, which agree with XRD results.

Generally, the mechanism that Mg(OH)2 removes Cd2+ is ion exchange [41,42]. Our CDs/Mg(OH)2 composite shows the same mechanism supported by experimental and characterized results, but its exchange rate is well controlled because of CDs introduction.

Firstly, the negatively charged CDs component helps to gather Cd2+ ions by adsorption, i.e. executing electrostatic attraction in long range and chemical coupling in short distance. The chemical adsorption simultaneously leads to a slow desorption of Cd(II) ions. This enables the subsequent ion exchange reaction in a controllable rate. The chemical coupling between CDs and Cd(II) ions has been proved by DFT calculations and fluorescent determination, which were presented in the last paragraph of the 3.2 section.

Secondly, the coupled CDs in the composite occupy some active sites of Mg(OH)2. This will decrease the contact possibility of the Mg(OH)2 component with Cd2+ ions. So we note that, in the initial stage, both adsorption and ion exchange rates are slower than those of the pure Mg(OH)2. See their results within 2 h in Fig. S8. As the reaction goes further, the CDs component in the composite starts slowly releasing Cd2+ that will react with Mg(OH)2. It is the reason that the reaction rate of the composite is faster than that of the pure Mg(OH)2 (after 4 h in Fig. S8).

In brief, controlling reaction rate is the key to recover Cd(OH)2 as highly-ordered 1D nanowires. Moreover, the synergistic effect of two sub-components results in high Cd2+ removal efficiency relative to Mg(OH)2. In contrast, due to the lack of rate controlling, the pure Mg(OH)2 does not afford 1D Cd(OH)2 (Fig. S7).

In order to explore the durability and reusability of CDs/Mg(OH)2, 12-cycle experiments of removing Cd2+ were carried out in 20 mg L−1 Cd2+ solution. As seen in Fig. 10g, the removal efficiency still retains 98.6% by the end of 12th cycle. This indicates that our composite is applicable to treating/purifying waste water.

In summary, we successfully prepared hierarchically petaloid-array CDs/Mg(OH)2 composite via a facile method. The Mg(OH)2 component is the brucite phase and has general layered structure, to which CDs attaches. The special morphology endows the composite with high specific surface area of 96.2 m2 g−1.

DFT calculation recognized the composite structure at the atomic level. The optimized separation between components of Mg(OH)2 and CDs falls within 2.5 and 2.9 Å, depending on choice of models. A weak chemical coupling is proved by interfacial interaction energies (-0.16 ~ -0.36 eV), infrared spectra, TEM and XPS. The interfacial interaction is dominated by orbital and electrostatic attractions. Specifically, it is attributed to π-type hydrogen bonds between two components.

The synergistic effect of Mg(OH)2 and CDs enables the composite to efficiently remove Cd2+ ions in a controllable rate. CDs/Mg(OH)2-60, for instance, affords the removal capacity of 1015.4 mg g−1. High removal efficiency (99%) is retained even after 12 cycles. Meanwhile, 1D Cd(OH)2 nanowires are recovered. The mechanism is proposed as the ion exchange in a controlled rate, where the CDs part slowly releases the gathered Cd2+ ions and the decreased active sites of Mg(OH)2 due to CDs occupation also enable to slow down the reaction.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Chinese Heilongjiang Province Science Fund for Distinguished Young Scholars (JC2017003). The Natural Science Foundations of China (21671060) and Heilongjiang Province (B201318) are also greatly acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.125961.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
